item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I removed the part they're referencing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait wait wait, What was that from? Am I missing something in the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Format screws have been around since the beginning, and with well over 4000 articles now you're not going to see less of them.

Let me put it this way: regardless of your intent or the fact that you liked the article, your original comment basically praised a part that wasn't in the article anymore, and wasnt pertinent to the story, while criticizing the actual structure and narrative gimmick in an annoyingly authoritative way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually don't know what edit you're talking about, I just said I liked the part with 1048. I liked most of this article btw, what I was talking about was PURELY the huge influx of format screws. I like format screws, they're usually done well. When EVERY third SCP is a format screw, it gets boring. Remember when that one guy wrote Percy Jackson and everyone loved it? How about when he wrote THE EXACT SAME STORY two more times with different mythologies, and no one noticed? When a market is over-saturated, it's over-saturated, it doesn't matter if it's good or not. I liked this damn article, the main crux of he argument was that EVERY article is this article.

I was being serious, by the way, I actually liked the part with 1048.

Edit: I see now that you took out the part with the bear, I had no idea you did that. I didn't stalk through your edits purely to harass you, I just read an earlier version of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't mind if you don't like the format screw I happened to go with, I don't mind if you do or don't like the article overall. What I do dislike is your comment doing exactly what I had mentioned in the edit where I removed the reference to 1048, the exact reason I had removed that part, which you had to have gone into the article history to read. And then following it up with "hey guys stop doing format screws because I, a person who has yet to contribute anything to the site, said so" which just reeks of entitlement and ignorance.

So yeah, I had originally type up a much longer response to your original comment, then scrapped it and replaced it with a much less polite, but still entirely accurate version. Not that it justifies it, but I was in the hospital recovering from back surgery at the time, so I hope you'll forgive me if I wasn't exactly feeling patient or generous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a mature, well thought out, valid argument.
I didn't even say I disliked the SCP, I'm just saying the site is flooded and oversaturrated with this exact same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everything about this comment sucks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May I suggest putting the footnote back? I think it was cool. I don't feel it was upvote-bait, just a little interesting detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool reference to the teddy, I actually REALLY loved that part, but… Can we stop with the weird meta stuff and the format screws? Or even just the "Haha I've hacked into the articles" SCPs? We've got dozens of all of them, they're not shocking or interesting anymore

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In 2008, I lost my son in a car accident.
Quit telling everyone I'm dead!
Sometimes I can still hear his voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kinda similar to SCP-451, but seems good enough on its own. Nice touch with the "I can't write in proper clinical style" bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had to mention the thing that makes EAR BEARS. Take my like ya disturbed jerk!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, honestly, that SCP-1048 shoutout was enough to bring +1 from me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scp-1048, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is. I blanked the page once, and added the bottom note and top note for other revisions. It was worth a shot, but I learned my lesson that it doesn't contribute much to the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Enjoyed this. A good mix between skip and tale. I really felt for the poor guy, and got a little jolt when I realized he was going to (or already has) killed his dad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, is there something interesting in the revision history currently? I noticed you deleted it a few times and added some in-universe comments to the revision notes. I didn't see any real differences in those revisions other than the comments.

I want to make sure I didn't miss anything from an enjoyable skip.

As I'm sure you've realized, trying to use revision history and other forum/wiki mechanics for in-universe exposition never works well. At least, I can't think of a time I've seen it done well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. This definitely doesn't feel as brutal for the subject at hand as something like Joe Schmoe or the articles shaggy linked but you manage to do enough and sink those hard hitting lines with great skill. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FYI for anyone actually seeing these words properly: The teddy bear is hiding in Sub-Level C, in the Restricted Access Wing. I think some of the equipment down there is masking your ability to detect it. I'm only alive because, get this, it's affected too! Bring something that can melt paperclips and staples, and maybe a strong base like potassium hydroxide. It has friends.

Even if this wasn't a brilliant article, I would have upvoted for this alone. Nice work, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't go wrong with a TrennerD brand product. What a fucking gut punch, too. "I want my life back and the only way to do that maybe is kill my dad." You have to wonder how mortified he'd be if he did it and nothing changed. Fuck.

This is a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was quite ready to downvote it, but I suppose I have a sort of flexibility in me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm agreeing with shaggy with regards to similarities to other antimemetics isolation style stories, but this felt sufficiently memorable to be distinct. Also that gut punch at the end hit hard.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, this was a risk I was aware of when the idea first struck me. The consensus I got in chat was that it could still stand on its own, as the effect was similar but not identical to other SCPs, and the format gave it a very different angle. It's a bit simplistic as far as where I took the core idea, and even in my own eyes it does feel weak in that regard. Unfortunately I'm rarely a strong enough writer to turn my ideas into something as impactful as I'd like them to be.

I've never linked 1048 to any of my other works, but most of my other skips are linked, and this felt like an okay place for a light hearted call back to my first article, as well as turning Dr. Carver into something slightly less one dimensional. It also shows that SCPs can be affected, and implies that SCP-3344 has been over every inch of the site in his search for an answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This unfortunately doesn't do enough to distance itself from the umpteen articles we have with similar stories, such as SCP-2329, that one 055 Tale, SCP-2283, the Series one researcher who can't perceive people, etc. It just kind of reiterates the very basic subject of isolation without elaborating on the elements that make it unique, which takes away from any impact it could have had and make sure it hard to become invested in. More focus on the relationship between him and his father would perhaps go a ways to help set this apart from the pack.

The second footnote was admittedly pretty cool, but is a silly aside that doesn't seem to contribute to the emotional punch I believe you may have been going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for giving it a second look, I'm glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first glance, I thought this was just a partially-finished article.

Turns out it's a really well written scip with a sad and interesting story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, at least someone finally found that damn teddy bear. That was getting embarrassing. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh cool, thank you. I appreciate you taking the time to read it even after I botched my edits and initial posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got it now — it took me the second read to understand that he showed up and wrote his own article, rather than being previously contained and forgotten, and after you pointed out that it's already in there, I think I've figured out what he plans to do. That detail sinking in makes it feel more like a self-contained story and less like a prologue, which turned out to be what got me over to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm, I feel that at the end, the implication of what he plans to do is pretty strong. I don't really know how to make it more obvious than with the line "If it works, I'll take care of Mom."

The format screw basically does push this into tale territory, but I feel like it works in context. There's no original containment procedures, since 3344 wrote his own article. I do think your point about giving more background on what he's been through is very valid. I just didn't want to stretch the monologue section of the SCP too far. I could add a table where the SCP records the events from his point of view, which would give it more SCP structure and hopefully address that issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that I had downvoted this, which must have happened while you were in the middle of posting it - I removed it now that the rest of this is here.

+ I like the idea a lot, though...
– hide block

…there's something I can't quite put my finger on that's preventing me from upvoting (so far; I change my mind a lot). We've had a few stories before about people who can't register in the awareness of other people, but the parental rejection angle and this basically being a kid rather than a trained agent or anything makes it stand out enough that it doesn't suffer from those comparisons IMO.

I think maybe there's just something missing — the implications that this character has something big planned, with no hints at all as to what that might be (that I caught) make it feel incomplete. This also so unstructured it feels more like a tale, which is no bad thing, but may be helping to throw me off. Some more traces of the "original" containment procedures being left in would both give background into what this ghost-person has been through so far and help it feel more like a proper SCP. [Edit: Read it again and got that this person was never contained, they've put themselves in because they're desperate to be noticed. That's neat.]

Either way, at its core this is pretty cool, and I'm excited to see where you're going with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I ignored the advice on how to handle blanking/editing the page so it'd show up in history. Because I am dumb. And really, the effect it shows in the history sucks/wasn't handled well. But I hope the article can stand on its own despite its rocky start.

EDIT: I forgot to thank everyone who provided feedback on this, because I'm a jerk. So, thank you to those who gave me the time of day, and helped make my first series 4 article a success.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been hoping for someone to post something like this for a while now.

+1
